data.title: Lululemon shares tumble on weak US demand, tariff woes
data.description:
Shares of Lululemon Athletica plunged more than 17% in premarket trading on Friday after the athletic wear maker slashed annual profit and sales forecasts, hurt by tepid U.S. demand and tariff costs.

The firm cut its annual profit forecast for the second straight quarter on Thursday as it grapples with pressure from losing market share to rivals, a volatile macroeconomic backdrop and tariffs that are hurting consumer discretionary spending.

Its shares have tumbled over 40% this year, with efforts to improve sales through weekly product launches having little effect as American shoppers head into the holiday season.

"We have let our product life cycles run too long within many of our core categories," CEO Calvin McDonald said in a post-earnings call on Thursday.

Comparable sales for its Americas segment, its largest, declined 1% while international sales rose 15%.

"The US drives the earnings and the US is fading fast here," Jefferies analyst Randal Konik said in a note.

"Rising competition won't stop either, which means LULU's earnings per-share is permanently impaired," Konik added.

The company expects annual profit per share between $12.77 and $12.97, down from $14.58 to $14.78 earlier.

It expects an impact of about $240 million on its 2025 gross profit from higher tariffs and the removal of the de minimis exemption.

The yogawear maker, which relies heavily on U.S. import tariff hotspots like Vietnam and mainland China for its raw materials and manufacturing, has been on the receiving end of heavy duties from President Donald Trump's trade policies.

Lululemon's forward price-to-earnings multiple, a common benchmark for valuing stocks, is 13.82, well below Nike's 39.21, according to data compiled by LSEG.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0843 GMT - Shares of European semiconductor companies are on the rise after Broadcom reported better-than-expected results for its fiscal third quarter. Shares of Dutch supplier of chip-making equipment ASML Holding are up 2%, while smaller rival ASM International gained 2.3%. Shares of BE Semiconductor Industries, the Dutch supplier of semiconductor assembly equipment, rise 2.1%. German chip maker Infineon Technologies logs a 1.9% gain. Shares of STMicroelectronics, which supplies to clients like Elon Musk's Tesla and Apple, rises 4.9%. Broadcom posted revenue of $15.95 billion for the quarter ended Aug. 3, above a FactSet estimate of $15.82 billion. Adjusted Ebitda came in at $10.70 billion, above a FactSet forecast of $10.47 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0834 GMT - ASML Holding is expected to log stronger growth in the coming years, UBS analysts write in a research note as they upgrade the stock to buy from neutral. The Dutch supplier of chip-making equipment said in July that it could no longer guarantee growth in 2026 amid uncertainty from President Trump's tariffs. However, the European Union has since then reached a deal that set a 15% tariff ceiling for EU semiconductor exports to the U.S. The analysts say ASML will provide 2026 guidance at 3Q or 4Q results that will give investors a clearer picture. They expect ASML to log a compound annual growth rate of 20% in EPS between 2026 and the end of the decade. ASML shares trade 1.8% higher at 659.20 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0832 GMT - Hong Kong shares closed higher, supported by solar and biotech stocks. The advance came as market sentiment on Chinese equities has improved. Xinyi Solar was the best-performing Hang Seng Index constituent, rising 7.5%. Sino Biopharmaceutical climbed 6.2% and Zijin Mining Group increased 5.85%. Among the few decliners, New Oriental Education & Technology fell 1.5% and JD Health International shed 0.85%. The benchmark Hang Seng Index ended 1.4% higher at 25417.98; the Hang Seng Tech Index rose 1.95%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0829 GMT - The latest data from the Halifax House Price Index points to a second-half recovery as mortgage approvals are back to prepandemic levels, Goodbody analyst Max Mulcahy says in a note. Still, although risks remain skewed to the downside--with weak public finances and the threat of tax rises and spending cuts in the U.K.'s autumn budget looming--Mulcahy expects a recovery and modest house price inflation in 2025. "The confirmation of Nov. 26th as Budget Day makes it unusually late and means we now have 12 weeks of leaks and elevated uncertainty which is likely to temper consumer-decision making over the coming months," Mulcahy says. Shares in Crest Nicholson are up 2.2%, followed by Barratt Redrow and Berkeley, both up 1.4%. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0826 GMT - A much weaker-than-expected U.S. nonfarm payrolls report at 1230 GMT would have a bigger dollar impact than much stronger-than-forecast data, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. A major payrolls miss should prompt markets to price in three Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts by year-end, he says. The market is fully priced for two cuts, LSEG data show. A major upside surprise could be treated with "much more caution as markets might start to doubt the credibility of data under the new leadership at the Bureau of Labor Statistics." President Trump recently nominated conservative economist EJ Antoni to replace BLS head Erika McEntarfer. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 98.055. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0823 GMT - Sanofi posted solid results of a new eczema drug trial that support the medicine's potential to hit annual peak sales of 5 billion euros, Berenberg analysts write. The trial results triggered a negative share reaction, but Sanofi's amlitelimab could still capture a 4% share of the eczema market in patients that already received an initial treatment, the analysts says. Amlitelimab's dosing schedule of four injections a year is attractive in a nascent treatment market with tremendous growth potential, making this a compelling opportunity for the French pharmaceutical company to deliver the safest treatment with superior dosing convenience, they add. "We consider this a good outcome in a market where stellar Dupixent data were unlikely to be repeated," Berenberg says, referring to the drug jointly developed by Sanofi and Regeneron. Sanofi shares rise 0.2%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - LVMH's fortunes in the months ahead should remain strongly correlated with industry trends, Jefferies analysts write in a note. It remains tough to construct a compelling case for luxury, or the French conglomerate, they say. The sector continues to face a slowdown in demand for high-end goods, particularly in China, which drove the industry's growth for years. Despite this, Jefferies expects LVMH's key fashion and leather goods division to report improved trends for the third quarter, with a less pronounced decline in sales than in the prior quarter. "The overall uncertainty about consumers' willingness to spend at the higher price points in the U.S. and Europe and the extent to which the less affluent Chinese consumer will rebound, will be critical," they say. Shares fall 1.2%. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0820 GMT - Broadcom shares surge Friday premarket after the maker of semiconductor and software products posted better-than-expected results for its fiscal third quarter. Broadcom stock closed 1.2% higher at $306.10 ahead of results on Thursday. Shares are up 7.3% at $328.48 Friday premarket. The company reported $15.95 billion in revenue for the quarter ended Aug. 3, up 22% on year. Analysts had forecast $15.82 billion in revenue, according to FactSet. Adjusted Ebitda came in at $10.70 billion, above a FactSet forecast of $10.47 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0819 GMT - Mainland China's stock market isn't likely to see a repeat of the boom and bust experienced in 2015, Julius Baer analyst Richard Tang writes in a note. Chinese regulators' views toward the stock market have become more constructive, Tang says. Over the past year, authorities have included supporting asset prices as part of their key policies, he adds. This is different from 10 years ago, when policies focused on minimizing downside risks in the stock market, Tang says. The stock market isn't as frothy as it was in 2015, Tang adds. The rally remains concentrated in a handful of growth sectors such as technology, and hasn't broadened to other sectors, Tang says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0816 GMT - It could be difficult for Berkeley Group to achieve the targets outlined in its 2035 strategy, Interactive Investor analyst Richard Hunter says in a research note. The U.K. housebuilder has much working in its favor but ultimately operates in a cyclical sector, which is currently constrained by the wider economic environment, he says. For potential buyers, the level of interest rates and general affordability are under some strain, he adds. "For the time being investors remain unconvinced by the recovery potential, with the market consensus stuck at a hold, albeit a strong one," Hunter says. Shares trade 1.7% higher at 3640 pence. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0813 GMT - Orsted's downgrade to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is a small negative ahead of Friday's vote on a proposed 60 billion Danish kroner rights issue, Jefferies analyst Ahmed Farman writes. The company lowered its full-year Ebitda guidance to between 24 billion and 27 billion kroner, from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously, driven by lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds during July and August. Orsted also mentioned it expects a construction delay at its Greater Changhua 2b project, which will also adversely affect Ebitda in 2025. Jefferies expects details on the rights issue in the coming days. Shares rise 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0808 GMT - U.S. non-farm payrolls data due to be released at 1230 GMT are expected to show that jobs growth cooled in August but wasn't severely weak, RBC Capital Markets strategists say in a note. RBC expects the data to show a "cool but not frozen 64,000 jobs added in August," they say. However, any significant revisions to jobs figures for previous months could add to market volatility, RBC says. The consensus forecast in a WSJ poll predicts that the U.S. economy created 75,000 jobs in August, after 73,000 jobs were created in July. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Japan's Ishiba may face leadership challenge on Monday
data.description:
Key points:
  • Ruling party to vote Monday on holding leadership race
  • Markets see strong chance of race, costing Ishiba his job
  • Leadership change could bring looser fiscal policy
  • Political uncertainty complicates BOJ's rate-hike timing
  • Ishiba says he plans to compile economic package this autumn

Lawmakers of Japan's ruling party will vote on Monday whether to hold an extraordinary leadership election that could oust embattled premier Shigeru Ishiba and have a significant impact on the world's fourth largest economy.

Concern over political uncertainty has led to a sell-off in the yen and Japanese government bonds (JGB) this week, with the yield on 30-year bonds hitting a record high on Wednesday.

While the policy paralysis around such a vote could add pain for an economy hit by U.S. tariffs, markets are focusing more on the chance of Ishiba's replacement by an advocate of looser fiscal and monetary policy such as Sanae Takaichi, who has criticised the Bank of Japan's interest rate hikes.

"The dominant market bet is for the LDP to hold a leadership race and for Ishiba, known as a fiscal hawk, to lose his job," said Katsutoshi Inadome, senior strategist at Sumitomo Mitsui Trust Asset Management.

"We'll likely see further selling in Japanese government bonds, as whoever succeeds Ishiba probably will be more open to loosening fiscal policy than him."

Ishiba has refused calls from within his Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) to step down and take responsibility for its July loss in an upper house election.

Staying in power, however, has become increasingly hard for him. Having lost a majority in both houses of parliament, his ruling coalition needs opposition support to pass legislation.

Wary of being attached to the unpopular premier, opposition parties have refused to cooperate. Even some of Ishiba's cabinet members are now calling for the choice of a new party leader.

Party rules require at least half the LDP members to request holding the race, which could follow this month or in October.

Such a contest could delay parliament's passage of an expected supplementary budget aimed at cushioning the blow to households from rising living costs.

ECONOMIC SUPPORT PACKAGE

Ishiba told reporters on Friday he plans to compile this autumn a package of measures to support the economy. Asked whether he would step down, he said: "All I can say is that my government will fulfil its responsibility to the public."

Eurasia Group gave odds of 60% that Ishiba would not survive.

"Ishiba's poor performance as party leader in lower and upper house elections and events in recent days, including former prime minister Aso Taro announcing his support for the special election, has turned the tide against Ishiba," said David Boling, director of Japan and Asian Trade at Eurasia.

That puts the focus on his potential successor.

While the LDP lacks a majority in parliament, its leader would still be the favourite to become next prime minister, due to a fragmented opposition.

The choice could affect not just the scale of fiscal stimulus, but the timing of the BOJ's next interest rate hike, analysts say.

Front-runners include 44-year-old Shinjiro Koizumi, a charismatic agriculture minister popular with the public, but whose views on economic policy are unknown.

Takaichi, who represents the LDP's right wing, is also seen by some analysts as a strong candidate to become Japan's first female prime minister. She lost the September leadership race to Ishiba in a run-off vote.

While several other candidates are expected to be in the fray, Takaichi stands out for her vocal opposition to BOJ rate hikes and calls to ramp up spending to underpin the fragile economy.

After ending a decade-long, massive stimulus programme last year, the BOJ raised interest rates to 0.5% in January on the view that Japan was on the cusp of sustainably meeting its inflation target of 2%.

Most economists polled by Reuters expect the central bank to raise rates again this year, with some betting on a hike in October.

"Under Takaichi, fiscal discipline will be out the window," Inadome said. "Markets still remember the time she made it to the run-up in September, and triggered huge selling in JGBs."

data.title: European stocks rise, long-dated yields ease ahead of US jobs data
data.description:
Key points:
  • European stocks rise, after S&P record high
  • Traders wait for U.S. jobs data, focus on rate cut expectations
  • Oil prices in third day of declines

European equities rose in early trading on Friday while long-dated bond yields eased, as expectations for U.S. rate cuts helped markets overcome concerns about fiscal deficits in various countries.

The S&P 500 hit a new all-time high on Thursday after weekly jobs data showed more jobless claims than expected. Asian stocks tracked Wall Street higher overnight.

At 0752 GMT, the MSCI World Equity Index was up 0.3% on the day EURONEXT:IACWI and Europe's STOXX 600 was up 0.4% , set to end the week slightly higher overall after recovering from a dip earlier in the week.

The FTSE 100 was up 0.3% CURRENCYCOM:UK100 and France's CAC 40 was up 0.2% .

Markets are all but certain of a quarter-point cut at the conclusion of the Fed's two-day rate-setting meeting on September 17, according to LSEG data.

Traders will be looking to the monthly U.S. jobs report due later in the session to confirm their expectations. A weaker labour market boosts stocks because it raises expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut rates.

"We have already seen yesterday the sign that perhaps there will be a weakening in jobs, paving the way for a done deal in September," said Francesco Sandrini, head of multi-asset strategies at Amundi.

Today's numbers "can confirm to some extent an easing stance of the Federal Reserve," Sandrini added.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell had already reinforced rate cut speculation with an unexpectedly dovish speech at last month's Fed symposium in Jackson Hole.

"Unless it's an absolutely stellar payrolls print, it's hard to see too much that's going to change the market away from locking in a September cut," said Ken Crompton, head of rates strategy at National Australia Bank.

"Beyond that, the terminal rate and how you get there, that's arguably still up for grabs."

Market sentiment has recovered in recent sessions after global stocks fell earlier this week and long-date bond yields in Europe hit their highest in years, as investors became concerned about the state of various countries' finances, particularly Britain and France.

Yields eased on Friday, with France's 30-year yield at 4.3944%, down from a peak of 4.523% on Wednesday (FR30YR=RR), and the UK's 30-year yield at 5.563%, after borrowing costs hit their highest level since 1998 earlier in the week.

The benchmark 10-year German yield was at 2.7122% (DE10YT=RR). German industrial orders unexpectedly fell in July, data on Friday showed.

Yields on 30-year Treasuries (US30YT=RR) were at 4.8593%, having touched their lowest in three weeks during Asian trading.

The U.S. dollar eased, with the dollar index down 0.2% at 98.054 , while the euro was up 0.2% at $1.1678 .

After months of negotiations, the U.S. signed a deal to impose lower auto tariffs on Japan. The dollar was down 0.3% against the yen, with the pair at 148.14 .

Oil prices were in their third day of declines. Brent crude futures fell 0.5% to $66.65 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude eased 0.6% to $63.05.

Gold was steady at $3,546.24, having hit a record peak of $3,578.50 on Wednesday.

data.title: Resolutions of the extraordinary general meeting of Ørsted A/S
data.description:
Ørsted A/S (Orsted)

Resolutions of the extraordinary general meeting of Ørsted A/S

05-Sep-2025 / 10:37 CET/CEST

5.9.2025 10:36:58 CEST | Ørsted A/S | Decisions of general meeting

Today, Ørsted A/S held an extraordinary general meeting, where the following decisions were adopted:

  • The proposed authorisation for the Board of Directors to increase Ørsted A/S’s share capital with pre-emptive rights for the existing shareholders was approved.
  • The existing authorisation for the Board of Directors to increase Ørsted A/S’s share capital without pre-emptive rights for the existing shareholders as set out in articles 4.2.1 and 4.2.2 of the Articles of Association was repealed.
  • The authorisation to register the resolutions of the general meeting was approved.

For further information, please contact:

Global Media RelationsTom Christiansen

+45 99 55 60 17

tomlc@orsted.com

Investor RelationsRasmus Keglberg Hærvig

+45 99 55 90 95

IR@orsted.com

About Ørsted

The Ørsted vision is a world that runs entirely on green energy. Ørsted develops, constructs, and operates offshore and onshore wind farms, solar farms, energy storage facilities, and bioenergy plants. Ørsted is recognised on the CDP Climate Change A List as a global leader on climate action and was the first energy company in the world to have its science-based net-zero emissions target validated by the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi). Headquartered in Denmark, Ørsted employs approx. 8,200 people. Ørsted's shares are listed on Nasdaq Copenhagen (Orsted). In 2024, the group's revenue was DKK 71.0 billion (EUR 9.5 billion). Visit orsted.com or follow us on Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram, and X.

Attachments

data.title: SNG: Ford eyes South Africa people-mover market with new Tourneo Custom models
data.description:
Imran Salie

Now that the dust has settled on the introduction of two new players to the local line-up Ford’s Tourneo Custom van, it’s a good time to have a look at what these two new vans offer and put into context the company’s decision to expand the range.

Ford has filled a gap in the market for prospective buyers looking to stand out from ordinary and not break the bank with the Tourneo Sport and Titanium X. Let me explain, the ordinary yet capable Tourneo Custom Trend is the Plain Jane that does its job at a reasonable price, but lacks the aura and road presence of say a Mercedes-Benz V-Class, but its siblings Sport and Titanium X siblings can stand up to a pricier luxury van from design standpoint at a lower price.

It's the Blue Oval’s attempt to capture some market share while the segment is one of the only categories expected to grow in the coming years, as explained by Ford Motor Company of South Africa’s CEO Neale Hill at the local launch of the Tourneo Custom Sport and Titanium X.

Market share year to date, June 2025, the Ford Tourneo Custom holds 6%, while the segment leader is the futuristically designed Hyundai Staria with 37%. The Mercedes-Benz V-Class follows with 24%. Other players the Ford Tourneo Custom is up against the Toyota Quantum, Volkswagen Caravelle and Kombi, and Mercedes-Benz Vito.

The Tourneo Custom Sport and Titanium X bring some serious upgrades that set them apart from the standard Trend. The Sport is all about dynamic flair, with a honeycomb-style grille, body-colour bumper, assertive rear spoiler, dual black-and-blue stripes, and 17-inch alloy wheels as standard.

Inside, it gets partial leather trim with blue accent stripes, a 13-inch touchscreen with Sync 4, and a suite of driver-assist features including adaptive cruise control and collision mitigation. Optional extras like the Mobile Office package make it practical for work while keeping that sporty, attention-grabbing look.

The Titanium X leans more into luxury, with Matrix LED headlamps, 19-inch multi-spoke wheels, and a classy front grille. Its cabin is a step up in comfort with heated second-row seats, a powered front passenger seat, and an optional near full-length panoramic roof via the Luxe Pack.

Add a 14-speaker B&O sound system, ambient lighting, and a 360-degree camera, and you’ve got a people-mover that blends practicality with high-end style. B

Both models keep the eight-seat layout and share the 2L single-turbo diesel engine, delivering 125kW and 390Nm, paired with an eight-speed automatic transmission and multiple drive modes for whatever urban or freeway adventure comes next.

Conclusion

I had the opportunity to drive both new models in KwaZulu-Natal with Ford SA in mixed driving conditions, such as urban streets and freeway cruising. The in-car feel is carried over from the LWB variant, ensuring that the driver has a similar experience to driving a large SUV from the cabin.

The single-turbocharged four-cylinder diesel engines proved themselves worthy on our drive route, successfully moving driver and passenger from point A to point B with not much fuss. It would, however, be interesting to see how well it does the same job with a full house of eight people seated. Both models sipped between 8 and 9L per 100km on our trip.

Visually, the Tourneo Custom Sport is just cool; the name is definitely befitting. Expect a decent amount of stares and thumbs up from strangers in this variant. The Tourneo Titanium X is less souped up visually, but leans more towards the high-end, classy design side. Both models successfully separate themselves from their established counterparts in the design department.

Overall, the new players are a welcome addition to the local Ford portfolio. They are solid people-movers that combine practicality with visual flair, offering buyers a choice between the Sport at R1 ,218,000 and the Titanium X at R, 264,000, both including Ford’s service plan, warranty, and roadside assistance packages.

data.title: India's Mahindra eyes best week in nearly 15 months after tax cuts
data.description:
** Shares of Mahindra & Mahindra climb nearly 12% in the week, top performing stock on blue-chip Nifty 50 index

** Automaker's shares set for best week since June 7, 2024, when markets bounced after India's general election results

** MAHM up 2.8% at record 3,577.80 rupees on Friday

** Investec estimates tax cuts would benefit 84% of MAHM's car volumes; its commercial vehicles to also benefit from lower tax rates

** MAHM and most of its carmaker rivals rated "buy" on avg, Tata Motors rated "hold" - data compiled by LSEG

** Stock's gains this week sets Nifty Auto index up for best week in four months

** Stock up ~12% YTD vs NIfty 50's ~5% climb

data.title: Hang Seng Jumps 1.4% at Close
data.description:
The Hang Seng surged 359 points or 1.4% to finish at 25,418 on Friday, snapping a three-day losing streak.

Optimism improved after Wall Street's S&P 500 hit a record high Thursday, with traders betting the Fed will cut rates this month amid further signs of a cooling U.S. labor market.

For the week, the index gained 1.4%, reversing the prior week’s loss, supported by a CNY 1 trillion liquidity injection from China’s central bank.

Still, upside was limited by the sharpest weekly drop in five months in mainland stocks as traders booked profits after a two-month rally and the end of China’s military parade.

All sectors participated in the rally, led by tech, consumers, and property.

Ubtech Robotics soared 4.4% on a CNY 250 million humanoid robot contract, while WuXi XDC Cayman climbed 3.6% after announcing a HKD 2.73 billion share sale.

Other top gainers included Horizon Robotics (9.5%), SMIC (6.0%), Hansoh Pharma (4.6%), and China Hongqiao (4.6%).

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0840 GMT - The European Central Bank is expected to leave interest rates on hold next Thursday and the market impact is likely to be limited, says Mediolanum's Niall Scanlon in a note. "We don't expect this meeting to be a market mover, as consensus points to a hold in rates from the ECB next week," the fixed income portfolio manager says. Economic data after the meeting will be more significant. Mediolanum expects data could weaken in the coming months as tariffs weighs on growth and German fiscal stimulus takes a while to feed through. Headline inflation is also forecast to be below target in 2026. The ECB could consider cutting rates again towards the end of 2025 or early in 2026, Scanlon says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0826 GMT - A much weaker-than-expected U.S. nonfarm payrolls report at 1230 GMT would have a bigger dollar impact than much stronger-than-forecast data, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. A major payrolls miss should prompt markets to price in three Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts by year-end, he says. The market is fully priced for two cuts, LSEG data show. A major upside surprise could be treated with "much more caution as markets might start to doubt the credibility of data under the new leadership at the Bureau of Labor Statistics." President Trump recently nominated conservative economist EJ Antoni to replace BLS head Erika McEntarfer. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 98.055. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0826 GMT - Long-dated yields are driven by growth and inflation expectations and by concerns about fiscal policy, says AXA Investment Managers' Chris Iggo in a note. Short-dated government bond yields on the other hand are influenced more by interest-rate expectations. This helps explain the recent trend for widening gaps between short-dated and long-dated yields as interest rates are reduced but longer-term economic worries persist. "The decline in global inflation rates since 2022 has allowed central banks to ease, a process that the market expects to continue," says the chair of AXA IM Investment Institute. Concerns that central banks might not meet inflation targets are helping to push long-dated yields higher, however, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0808 GMT - U.S. non-farm payrolls data due to be released at 1230 GMT are expected to show that jobs growth cooled in August but wasn't severely weak, RBC Capital Markets strategists say in a note. RBC expects the data to show a "cool but not frozen 64,000 jobs added in August," they say. However, any significant revisions to jobs figures for previous months could add to market volatility, RBC says. The consensus forecast in a WSJ poll predicts that the U.S. economy created 75,000 jobs in August, after 73,000 jobs were created in July. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0757 GMT - An upcoming confidence vote called by French Prime Minister Francois Bayrou over his budget plans is unlikely to have a significant impact on the euro, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. Bayrou is expected to lose the vote on Monday and a "realistic scenario" is that President Emmanuel Macron would then choose a new centrist or centre-right prime minister to deliver a watered-down fiscal consolidation package, he says. Political uncertainty will remain elevated but it probably won't be enough to trigger uncontrolled moves in French government bonds or the euro as a no-confidence outcome is largely priced in, he says. The euro rises 0.2% to $1.1672. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - Bitcoin rises to its highest in one week ahead of key U.S. jobs data at 1230 GMT as markets bet on the Federal Reserve resuming interest-rate cuts from the September 17 meeting. Weaker-than-expected ADP private payrolls data Thursday fuelled expectations for another soft nonfarm payroll report on Friday. This would strengthen the case for the Fed to cut rates later this month and likely beyond, potentially boosting risky assets including bitcoin. Bitcoin rises 2.1% to one-week high of $112,876, LSEG data show, extending gains after breaking back above $112,000. However, it remains well below the record high of $124,480 reached in mid-August, having recently fallen as traders took profits. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0747 GMT - The Indonesian rupiah could receive support from expectations for Fed rate cuts later this month, which is expected to renew broad-based weakness in the dollar, UOB's global economics and markets research team writes in a report. The rupiah has depreciated by 1.5% over the past two months, largely due to consecutive rate cuts by Bank Indonesia, UOB says. BI's dovish stance has weighed on the rupiah, making it one of the region's underperformers. The rupiah could also be weighed by negative sentiment in the near term from recent public protests, UOB says. BI's firm commitment to maintain currency stability is likely to help contain volatility. USD/IDR is little changed at 16,415, LSEG data shows. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0742 GMT - Gold prices edge higher in early trade after pausing from a record-breaking rally as traders locked in profits ahead of the release of key U.S. nonfarm payrolls data. Futures are up 0.1% to $3,610.40 a troy ounce and on track for a 2.6% weekly gain, after hitting a new high of $3,640.10/oz earlier this week. The precious metal's surge was largely driven by expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its next meeting, concerns about the U.S. central bank's independence following President Trump's move to fire Fed governor Lisa Cook, and persistent geopolitical risks. Now, "the biggest risk from a market perspective is an upside surprise to today's payrolls number," says Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB. "If rate cut bets get scaled back, then inflation fears could ease, which may hurt the world's most famous inflation hedge." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, fall along with their eurozone peers ahead of key U.S. non-farm payrolls data due at 1230 GMT. Weak jobs data could increase expectations for U.S. interest-rate cuts in the coming months, starting in September. They come after U.S. job openings data and ADP private payrolls data came in weaker than expected. "A reading around consensus would be a sign that the U.S. labor market is slowing down sharply," XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. Gilts shrug off U.K. retail sales data showing a solid monthly rise during July but a weaker-than-expected annual increase. Ten-year gilt yields fall 3 basis points to last trade at 4.707%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0719 GMT - Jefferies' bigger worry about France is about ratings rather than about politics. Fitch Ratings is due to review France on Sept. 12, and it already has a negative outlook. "If political uncertainty persists, we see a case for Fitch downgrading France," Jefferies' global economist Mohit Kumar says in a note. However, Moody's Ratings or S&P Global Ratings are unlikely to downgrade France, according to Jefferies. "Risk remains that if France loses the coveted AA level rating (currently AA-) from two or more rating agencies, it could lead to forced selling from some of the Asian accounts." For now, Jefferies remains bearish on French bonds and expects the 10-year French-German bond-yield spread to rise towards 90 basis points, from 77 basis points currently, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0702 GMT - Sterling edges higher after data showed U.K. retail sales rose slightly more than expected during July. Retail sales increased 0.6% in July compared to the previous month, above the 0.5% forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. The data come after Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey said earlier in the week that the timing of future interest-rate cuts is now more uncertain amid concerns about inflationary pressures. The market is pricing in a 36% chance of another rate cut by year-end, LSEG data show. Sterling rises to an intraday high of $1.3471 after the data, from $1.3460 beforehand. The euro falls to an intraday low of 0.8662, pounds from 0.8673 previously. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0637 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening slightly higher, with IG calling the OMXS30 up 0.3% at around 2615. The market is eagerly awaiting today's U.S. labor market data and how this will affect expectations of the Fed's interest-rate cuts, SEB's chief emerging markets strategist Erik Meyersson writes. SEB expects another month of employment growth of 75,000--in line with consensus--and unemployment to continue to rise slightly. "We think this will be enough for a 25 basis-point cut from the Fed in September." The money market is now pricing in at least two interest-rate cuts by the Fed before the end of the year, he says. OMXS30 closed at 2607.54, OMXN40 at 2293.02 and OBX at 1556.28. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Hong Kong Shares Rebound; Solar, Biotech Stocks Lead Gains — Market Talk
data.description: Hong Kong shares closed higher, supported by solar and biotech stocks. The advance came as market sentiment on Chinese equities has improved. Xinyi Solar was the best-performing Hang Seng Index constituent, rising 7.5%. Sino Biopharmaceutical climbed 6.2% and Zijin Mining Group increased 5.85%. Among the few decliners, New Oriental Education & Technology fell 1.5% and JD Health International shed 0.85%. The benchmark Hang Seng Index ended 1.4% higher at 25417.98; the Hang Seng Tech Index rose 1.95%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Indian miner GMDC hits record high; eyes best week in more than 3-1/2 years
data.description:
** Gujarat Mineral Development Corporation jumps 12% to record high of 510 rupees; set for sharpest intraday pct gain in seven weeks

** Stock soars ~26% this week; set for best weekly gain since week ended January 14, 2022

** Week's gains driven by government approving 15 bln-rupee ($171.4 mln) incentive scheme on Wednesday to promote critical mineral recycling in country

** Stock rose 4.5% on Wednesday before announcement in anticipation of approval, fell marginally on Thursday

** 677,001 shares traded in 5 block deals at 488.5-511.5 rupees, data compiled by LSEG showed

** Overall more than 26.5 mln shares traded, 5.8x 30-day moving avg; stock sees busiest session since July 18

** YTD, GMDC gains 58%

($1 = 87.5060 Indian rupees)

data.title: Temenos Shares Dive After CEO Departure
data.description:
By Aimee Look

Temenos shares fell following the departure of its chief executive, Jean-Pierre Brulard, who held the role for 16 months.

Shares in the Swiss banking software company were trading 13% lower at 61.75 Swiss francs in European morning trade on Friday, wiping out the stock's gains since the start of the year.

Temenos said on Thursday its chief financial officer, Takis Spiliopoulos, would take over the CEO role on an interim basis, while the board looks for a permanent successor.

The decision raises questions about the company's long-term strategy, as it seems to stem from a difference in views between the board and Brulard, analysts at Jefferies wrote.

"Our interpretation is that the CEO wanted a bolder approach, while the board wanted less disruption to teams and more stability," the analysts said.

The company reiterated its short-term guidance, but volatility at the top might hinder investor sentiment, the analysts added.

Write to Aimee Look at aimee.look@wsj.com
